Notes about meta:
< meta http-equiv ="refresh" content ="30">
The http-equiv attribute provides an HTTP header for the information/value of the content attribute. The http-equiv attribute can be used to simulate an HTTP response header. Value Description
content- security- policy
Specifies a content policy for the document. Example: <meta http-equiv="content-security-policy" content="default-src 'self'">
content-type Specifies the character encoding for the document. Example: <meta http-equiv="content-type" content="text/html; charset=UTF-8">
default-style Specified the preferred style sheet to use. Example: <meta http-equiv="default-style" content=" the document's preferred stylesheet "> Note: The value of the content attribute above must match the value of the title attribute on a link element in the same document, or it must match the value of
refresh Defines a time interval for the document to refresh itself.

in the same document, or it must match the value of

What Is ECMAScript ?

How to build a Backend 1. Define Your Project: o Clearly outline the project's purpose, target audience, and core functionalities. o Identify the data you'll be handling, how it will be structured, and the operations required (CRUD: Create, Read, Update, Delete). 2. Choose the Right Tech Stack: o Select programming languages (Python, JavaScript, Ruby, Java, etc.) based on your familiarity and project requirements. o Consider using frameworks like Django, Express.js, Ruby on Rails, or Spring Boot to accelerate development. o Choose a database system (SQL or NoSQL) depending on your data structure and query needs. 3. Design the Database Schema: o Create a logical structure for your data, defining tables (for SQL) or collections (for NoSQL). o Determine relationships between data entities (one-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-many). o Normalize your data to avoid redundancy and improve data integrity. 4. Develop the API: o Define endpoints for different functionalities (e.g., user registration, data retrieval, updates). o Choose an API architecture (REST, GraphQL, or a combination) based on your project's needs. o Implement data validation and error handling. 5. Implement Business Logic: o Write code to handle core functionalities and calculations. o Consider using design patterns for code organization and reusability. 6. Ensure Security: o Protect user data with encryption and secure authentication methods. o Implement input validation and sanitization to prevent attacks like SQL injection and cross-site scripting (XSS). o Regularly update dependencies and address security vulnerabilities. 7. Test Thoroughly: o Write unit tests to verify individual components. o Conduct integration tests to ensure different parts work together seamlessly. o Perform load testing to assess performance under heavy usage. 8. Deploy to Production: o Choose a hosting platform or cloud provider (AWS, GCP, Azure). o Configure servers, databases, and application deployment. o Implement monitoring and logging for performance and troubleshooting.

Additional Considerations: • Scalability: Design your backend to handle increasing user loads and data volumes. • Performance: Optimize database queries, caching, and code for speed. • Error Handling: Provide informative error messages and graceful failure mechanisms. • Documentation: Create clear documentation for the API and backend logic. • Version Control: Use Git or similar tools to manage code changes and collaborate effectively. In the Internship: Understanding the Challenge You're transitioning from a familiar language and framework to a new stack. This means learning new syntax, concepts, and tools. But the core principles of building backend systems remain the same: • Data management: Storing, retrieving, and updating information. • User authentication: Protecting access to data. • API design: Creating endpoints for data interaction. • Logic implementation: Handling business rules and calculations. Your First Steps 1. Master Laravel Basics: o Installation: Set up a Laravel project. o Routing: Understand how to define URL patterns and their corresponding controllers. o Controllers: Learn how to handle incoming requests and return responses. o Models: Create models to interact with the database. o Views: (Optional for now) Learn how to create user interfaces, but for a backend focus, this can come later. o Eloquent ORM: Familiarize yourself with Laravel's ORM for database interactions. 2. Database Design: o Entity-Relationship Diagrams (ERDs): Visualize how data is related. o Migrations: Use Laravel's migration system to create database tables. o Seeders: Populate your database with initial data for testing. 3. Core CRM Features: o Contact Management: Create models for contacts, companies, addresses, etc. o Lead Management: Define lead stages, sources, and related data. o Opportunity Management: Model sales pipelines, deals, and probabilities. o User Management: Implement user roles, permissions, and authentication. 4. Leverage Laravel Features: o Authentication: Use Laravel's built-in authentication scaffolding to handle user logins and registrations. o Validation: Validate user input to prevent errors and security vulnerabilities. missions, and authentication. 4. Leverage Laravel Features: o Authentication: Use Laravel's built-in authentication scaffolding to handle user logins and registrations. o Validation: Validate user input to prevent errors and security vulnerabilities. o Requests: Handle incoming requests and data validation using form requests. o Testing: Write unit and integration tests to ensure code quality. Example Structure

Bash crm/ ├── app/ │ ├── Http/ │ │ ├── Controllers/ │ │ │ ├── ContactController.php │ │ │ ├── LeadController.php │ │ │ ├── OpportunityController.php │ │ │ └── UserController.php │ │ ├── Middleware/ │ │ ├── Requests/ │ │ └── Routes/ │ ├── Models/ │ │ ├── Contact.php │ │ ├── Lead.php │ │ ├── Opportunity.php │ │ └── User.php │ ├── Providers/ │ └── Console/ ├── config/ ├── database/ ├── public/ ├── resources/ ├── routes/ ├── storage/ ├── tests/ ├── vendor/ └── .env Additional Tips • Break down the problem: Start with a small, manageable feature and gradually expand. • Learn by doing: Experiment with different approaches and iterate. • Seek help: Don't hesitate to ask your colleagues or online communities for assistance. • Leverage Laravel's documentation: It's a valuable resource. Remember, building a CRM is a learning process. Focus on understanding the core concepts and gradually adding complexity. With dedication and practice, you'll be able to create a robust and efficient CRM system. Hints in Backend: 1. When there is a problem in fetch that can be due to an error in the app.post function in the server side, you must check where the error there and add many console.log to print the data so you can know where the data lost. 2. When there is a problem in the appearing of any object, you must check objects that appear in the DOM, and if you want to pass data between 2 views, you can use local storage What is a back-end? • The back-end is all of the technology required to process the incoming request and generate and send the response to the client. This typically includes three major parts:

• 1. The server. This is the computer that receives requests. 2.The app. This is the application running on the server that listens for requests, retrieves information from the database, and sends a response. 3. The database. Databases are used to organize and persist data. How to build a Backend for a Website? Backend involves 1. Define your needs: • how it should work and what core functionalities it should have. • What functionalities will you implement in the future? • How many users will use your app? 2. Choose the right tools • Programming language: This decision will have an effect on all future possibilities and limitations . • API: it is time to specify the type of API (Public, Private, Partner, Composite) and its architecture (REST, SOAP, RPC, GraphQL). It is the massenger that takes request and tells the system what o you want to do and then returns response back to you, it is like a waiter. • API Documentation. • Backend is responsible for creating APIs (Application Programming Interfaces) that allow communication between different components of a system. APIs enable integration with third-party services and ensure a seamless user experience. • Operating system : Then it is good to find the best operating system . Here it is a simple choice: Linux or Windows . In the case of Linux, we need to specify the distribution (RedHat, Fedora, Debian, Ubuntu, etc.) and its version . To avoid being locked into a single operating system, developers often use Docker – a tool that allows them to create a small virtual system just for development purposes. • Building Data base : 3. Coding • folder structure: MCV structure. • paradigm to choose (functional or object-oriented programming) • methodology: TDD (Test-Driven Development), BDD (Behavior-Driven Development), and DDD (Domain-Driven Development). • follow a few principles like SOLID (five code design principles), KISS (Keep It Simple Stupid), and DRY (Don’t Repeat Yourself). 4. Deployment: deploy the code into the world • we have many cloud platforms like AWS (Amazon Web Services), Google Cloud, Firebase, DigitalOcean, Heroku, etc. • DevOps have to configure all services you need for the project, eg. ’t Repeat Yourself). 4. Deployment: deploy the code into the world • we have many cloud platforms like AWS (Amazon Web Services), Google Cloud, Firebase, DigitalOcean, Heroku, etc. • DevOps have to configure all services you need for the project, eg. compute cloud/ engine, storage and database. In this step, we usually set up two environments: prod (what all can use) and stage (to show changes to the client and for testing purposes).

5. CI/CD: Continuous Integration / Continues Delivery. While CI is responsible for building code and testing it (with automatic tests), CD takes care of the build delivery to the server. Again, we have plenty of tools that can help us like eg. Jenkins, GitLab, CircleCI, TeamCity, Bamboo, etc. 6. Monitoring: To minimize the risk of sudden interruption on the backend, DevOps uses monitoring systems to analyze the utility of resources. Many cloud platforms have built-in solutions but if there is a need to use custom tools we can choose one of these: Sematex Monitoring, Prometheus and Grafana, Dynatrace, Datadog, etc. Important things to have Knowelage about: 1. Communication Protocols Communications protocols link the frontend to the backend • TCP o is a stream-based connection-oriented protocol . o provides reliable delivery at a cost of connection setup and retransmission • UDP o a message based and connectionless. o starts faster but doesn’t have guaranteed delivery. • There are multiple protocols we have to determine the protocol we will use according to project requirements. • HTTP protocol is built on top or TCP/UDP 2. Accesability along with security compilance (Knowledge of Web Security ). • You can secure communications with encryption or TLS. • You can prevent network intrusion with firewall rules and proper network configuration. You can study common software vulnerabilities and protect against denial of service attacks. As a software engineer, it is important to be familiar with the different types of security risks and how to mitigate them. • One type of security risk is a man-in-the-middle attack. This is where an attacker intercepts communication between two parties and tries to eavesdrop or modify the data being exchanged. To prevent this type of attack, it is important to encrypt and authenticate the communicated parties using Transport Layer Security (TLS). • Another type of security risk is a denial of service attack. This is where an attacker tries to prevent legitimate users from accessing a service by flooding it with requests or through finding a way to crash the backend by sending a special payload.

Another type of security risk is a denial of service attack. This is where an attacker tries to prevent legitimate users from accessing a service by flooding it with requests or through finding a way to crash the backend by sending a special payload. To prevent this type of attack, it is important to have a firewall or a layer 7 DDOS protection layer in place to block illegitimate requests. Cloudflare has great services to detect DDOS traffic. This is of course made possible through layer 7 inspection. • There is a client side security attacks such as XSS (cross side scripting), there are server side security attacks such as SQL injection • Using HTTPS: HTTPS is a secured HTTP. When data is sent using HTTPS, it is protected via the Transport Layer Security protocol, which has three layers of protection: o Encryption: Way of securing data along with a password or “key” which is used to decrypt the information. o Data Integrity: When data integrity is secure, information stored in a database is complete, accurate, and reliable for a lifetime. o Authentication: This method protects against attacks and builds user trust which response to business growth.

• Implementing secure backup and recovery: Data loss could be the major threat that could lead to security breaches. To avoid this, make sure to implement a backup process. This could be implemented in many ways: o Protecting media and content from theft or destruction. o Periodically testing the backups. o Apply a two-person rule so that one person cannot access the content or media without the permission of another article. o and various other methods include XSS Attacks, Software updates, and SQL Injection. 3. Web Server: • Web servers deliver static or dynamic content served on top of the HTTP protocol. • If you build a Web API you may spin up your own web server in your language of choice or use a web framework such as Express or Django. • The internal architecture of web servers can also differ. Web servers can be single or multithreaded. They can have one thread or multiple listener threads. There are many ways client connections can be accepted and distributed among the threads • Examples of off-the-shelf web servers are Apache Tomcat, Apache httpd, and NGINX . The latter can act as both a web server and a proxy. • Modern web servers support both HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/2. HTTP/3 is slowly getting support as its newer protocol. Configuring your web server with the appropriate protocol is critical for performance and resilience of your backend application; And it all depends on the environment. For example, Do you expect multiple concurrent requests from the same client? or do you expect fewer requests but from many clients? Based on that, you can pick multiplexing HTTP/ 2 or vanilla simple HTTP/1.1. • The web server runs websites, it stores, processes, and delivers (response) web pages to the user’s request. When the user makes a request by the web server, it is accepted by an HTTP server which finds and sends back the content to the browser through HTTP. Some examples of web servers are Apache and NGINX and express js which are open-source platforms used to deliver content as per requests made. • When you use Express.js, the web server is essentially built by: 1. Node.js: Provides the underlying HTTP server functionality. 2.

ers are Apache and NGINX and express js which are open-source platforms used to deliver content as per requests made. • When you use Express.js, the web server is essentially built by: 1. Node.js: Provides the underlying HTTP server functionality. 2. Express.js: Handles routing, middleware, and other web application logic on top of Node.js. Like that: • const server = http.createServer((req, res) => { res.statusCode = 200; res.setHeader('Content-Type', 'text/plain'); res.end('Hello, World!\n'); }); 4. Proxy: • Proxies are becoming increasingly popular in the engineering world, especially with the introduction of micro-services. The main purpose of a proxy is it receives requests from a client and forward the requests to backend servers. The proxy hides the network layer identity of the original client from the destination server.

• There are two levels of proxying: layer 4 and layer 7 proxying. Layer 4 proxying works at the transport layer, while layer 7 proxying works at the application level. Each layer provides different capabilities and can be used for different purposes. Layer 7 proxying require the proxy to understand the application protocol while layer 4 proxying works with any application protocol because it works at the transport layer (TCP or UDP). • You might have heard the forward proxy and reverse proxy used interchangeably. Both the forward proxy and the reverse proxy, receive requests from clients and forward the request to a backend. In the forward proxy, the client explicitly asks for a particular backend server and the proxy fulfills this request. Forward proxies must be configured in the client network proxy section for them to work. In the reverse proxy the client doesn’t know the final backend server, to the client the final destination is the reverse proxy. The client doesn’t know there are more servers on the backend. • A common uses cases of proxies are caching, API gateways, authentication, load balancing and much more. For example a content delivery network (CDN) is a reverse proxy that sends request to the origin backends. Fiddler or MITM are proxies configured on the client side and all requests* get sent to them first. Service meshes are essentially a proxy and a reverse proxy. Learn more about proxies here. 5. Authontication : there is a detailed website in the references part. 6. App scaling 7. Hosting Environment 8. System Design 9. Containerization & Testing • Containerization is the process of packaging software code with all the necessary elements like frameworks, libraries, and other dependencies, to make services isolated from each other in a container. This process is done by the backend developers to run a container easily. Docker and Kubernetes are the most used containers. • Docker is an open-source containerization platform, that enables developers to package applications into containers. It is similar to a virtual machine but much more efficient. Its basic use of it is it allows the applications to run in different environments.

an open-source containerization platform, that enables developers to package applications into containers. It is similar to a virtual machine but much more efficient. Its basic use of it is it allows the applications to run in different environments. • Kubernetes often abbreviated as “K8s”, automates deploying and managing cloud-native applications using public cloud platforms. 10. Deployment • When the testing passes, developers move to the deployment where they can see the working of their application. It can also be referred to as a live environment. This process includes 5 steps: Planning, development, testing, deploying, and monitoring. • Planning: When multiple developers are working on the same project, a deployment plan is a necessity. Using this step, you avoid conflicts between changes made making sure the deployment process is as smooth and easy. This could lead to the quicker and more efficient development of the application. • Development: Once, the planning is done, actual development takes place. This is the step where you gather ideas and prioritization requirements. Then, the management of the complete application can be moved to testing.

• Testing: Testing is a crucial step to ensure there are no bugs before moving it to the production level. Testing is possible only when you deploy it to various environments to check whether it fits in or not. Check for the bugs and if any, make sure to remove them and then move to the deployment process. • Deploying: Seeing your application working live, with no bugs, and in an optimized way gives satisfaction to the effort made. It means pushing changes or updates from one environment to another. • Monitoring: Constant monitoring is required to check whether the website is working well or not. There might be a chance that issues may occur after deploying as well, so keep an eye out for the proper functioning, and the time for release should be planned so that minimum users would have viewed and changes can be made to see the optimized web application. 11. Cloud Providers o Everything is making its way to the cloud, be it software, applications, services, or products. Cloud computing. It is Web-based computing that permits businesses and individuals to consume computing resources such as virtual machines, databases, processing, services, storage, and events, and gives you a pay-as-you-go service. Cloud computing can be divided into three major categories, Software as a Service (SaaS), Platform as a Service (PaaS), and Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS). The most used Cloud Services are: o Amazon Web Services (AWS) o Microsoft Azure o Google Cloud o Alibaba Cloud o IBM Cloud 12. Compliance and Regulations: • Backend developers implement features and processes to ensure that applications comply with legal regulations and industry standards, such as data protection laws. 13. Data Validation: Backend is responsible for validating data before it is stored in the database. This ensures data integrity and prevents the storage of incorrect or inconsistent data. 14. Scalability: Backend architecture plays a key role in the scalability of an application. Well- designed backend systems can easily scale to handle increased loads and user traffic. 15. Support for Multiple Platforms : Backend logic ensures consistency and compatibility across various platforms and devices.

scalability of an application. Well- designed backend systems can easily scale to handle increased loads and user traffic. 15. Support for Multiple Platforms : Backend logic ensures consistency and compatibility across various platforms and devices. This allows users to access the application from different devices without compromising the user experience. 16. Updates and Maintenance: Backend development facilitates easier updates and maintenance of applications. Changes to the application logic can be implemented on the server without requiring updates to individual client devices. 17. Optimization: Backend developers optimize code and databases to improve application performance. This includes optimizing queries, implementing caching strategies, and reducing response times. 18. Message Formats

• Message formats go hand in hand with communication protocols; They describe in- wire format of the message being sent. They usually broken down into two types human readable and non-human readable. Examples are XML, JSON and protocol buffers. • When a client sends a message to a backend, it needs to serialize the message from the language data structure to the on-wire message format. When the backend receives the message it needs to then deserialize the message from this format to the language data structure. 19. Message Sytems like kafka and RabbitQA 20. What is a server? • A server is simply a computer that listens for incoming requests. Though there are machines made and optimized for this particular purpose, any computer that is connected to a network can act as a server. In fact, you will often use your very own computer as server when developing apps. Project Folders: 1. Models: has the schema models and relations. 2. Routes: includes all routes and has a file for each route type. 3. Controllers: Our routes get requests and pass these requests to the suitable controller fn , then it uses models to get data and pass these data into a view, and we reference those controllers fns in our routes file, and then the view renders these data into its template and it gets sent back to the browser. • This folder will include files according to each routig file , then the const blog =require('../models/’) will be moved to this file. 4. Views folders have the .ejs files 5. app.js file 6. server.js file Notes: 7. URI: A URI (Uniform Resource Identifier) is a string that refers to a resource.The most common are URLs, which identify the resource by giving its location on the Web. URNs, by contrast, refer to a resource by a name, in a given namespace, such as the ISBN of a book. 8. What are the core functions of the app? The server runs an app that contains logic about how to respond to various requests based on the HTTP verb and the Uniform Resource Identifier (URI). The pair of an HTTP verb and a URI is called a route and matching them based on a request is called routing. Some of these handler functions will be middleware.

ond to various requests based on the HTTP verb and the Uniform Resource Identifier (URI). The pair of an HTTP verb and a URI is called a route and matching them based on a request is called routing. Some of these handler functions will be middleware. In this context, middleware is any code that executes between the server receiving a request and sending a response. These middleware functions might modify the request object, query the database, or otherwise process the incoming request. Middleware functions typically end by passing control to the next middleware function, rather than by sending a response.

Eventually, a middleware function will be called that ends the request-response cycle by sending an HTTP response back to the client. What is a Proxy?A proxy is essentially an intermediary that acts on behalf of another. General Concept: Imagine you're too busy to go to the grocery store. You ask your friend to go for you. Your friend becomes your proxy - they act on your behalf to purchase the groceries. Proxy in Computing: In the digital world, a proxy server acts as an intermediary between your computer and the internet. It handles your internet requests and responds on your behalf. Why use a proxy? Privacy: Hides your IP address, making it harder for websites to track you. Security: Can filter malicious content or protect against attacks. Caching: Stores copies of frequently accessed web pages to speed up loading times. Circumventing restrictions: Can be used to access blocked websites or content. Types of Proxies:
HTTP Proxy: Handles HTTP requests. SOCKS Proxy: More flexible, allowing for different protocols. Reverse Proxy: Directs traffic to different servers based on rules.

xy: More flexible, allowing for different protocols. Reverse Proxy: Directs traffic to different servers based on rules. Web Server
Directly serves content: Typically handles static content (HTML, CSS, images) and dynamic content generated by applications running on the same server. Focus: Primarily concerned with delivering content to clients. Proxy Server
Intermediary: Acts as a middleman between clients and backend servers. Additional functionalities: Often provides additional services like load balancing, caching, security, and filtering. Flexibility: Can be configured to forward requests to different backend servers based on various criteria. To summarize:
A web server is primarily focused on serving content. A proxy server is more versatile, offering additional features beyond simple request forwarding. While a web server can handle basic request forwarding, a proxy server provides more advanced capabilities and is often used in complex architectures.

How to build a Backend 1. Define Your Project: o Clearly outline the project's purpose, target audience, and core functionalities. o Identify the data you'll be handling, how it will be structured, and the operations required (CRUD: Create, Read, Update, Delete). 2. Choose the Right Tech Stack: o Select programming languages (Python, JavaScript, Ruby, Java, etc.) based on your familiarity and project requirements. o Consider using frameworks like Django, Express.js, Ruby on Rails, or Spring Boot to accelerate development. o Choose a database system (SQL or NoSQL) depending on your data structure and query needs. 3. Design the Database Schema: o Create a logical structure for your data, defining tables (for SQL) or collections (for NoSQL). o Determine relationships between data entities (one-to-one, one-to-many, many-to-many). o Normalize your data to avoid redundancy and improve data integrity. 4. Develop the API: o Define endpoints for different functionalities (e.g., user registration, data retrieval, updates). o Choose an API architecture (REST, GraphQL, or a combination) based on your project's needs. o Implement data validation and error handling. 5. Implement Business Logic: o Write code to handle core functionalities and calculations. o Consider using design patterns for code organization and reusability. 6. Ensure Security: o Protect user data with encryption and secure authentication methods. o Implement input validation and sanitization to prevent attacks like SQL injection and cross-site scripting (XSS). o Regularly update dependencies and address security vulnerabilities. 7. Test Thoroughly: o Write unit tests to verify individual components. o Conduct integration tests to ensure different parts work together seamlessly. o Perform load testing to assess performance under heavy usage. 8. Deploy to Production: o Choose a hosting platform or cloud provider (AWS, GCP, Azure). o Configure servers, databases, and application deployment. o Implement monitoring and logging for performance and troubleshooting.

Additional Considerations: • Scalability: Design your backend to handle increasing user loads and data volumes. • Performance: Optimize database queries, caching, and code for speed. • Error Handling: Provide informative error messages and graceful failure mechanisms. • Documentation: Create clear documentation for the API and backend logic. • Version Control: Use Git or similar tools to manage code changes and collaborate effectively. In the Internship: Understanding the Challenge You're transitioning from a familiar language and framework to a new stack. This means learning new syntax, concepts, and tools. But the core principles of building backend systems remain the same: • Data management: Storing, retrieving, and updating information. • User authentication: Protecting access to data. • API design: Creating endpoints for data interaction. • Logic implementation: Handling business rules and calculations. Your First Steps 1. Master Laravel Basics: o Installation: Set up a Laravel project. o Routing: Understand how to define URL patterns and their corresponding controllers. o Controllers: Learn how to handle incoming requests and return responses. o Models: Create models to interact with the database. o Views: (Optional for now) Learn how to create user interfaces, but for a backend focus, this can come later. o Eloquent ORM: Familiarize yourself with Laravel's ORM for database interactions. 2. Database Design: o Entity-Relationship Diagrams (ERDs): Visualize how data is related. o Migrations: Use Laravel's migration system to create database tables. o Seeders: Populate your database with initial data for testing. 3. Core CRM Features: o Contact Management: Create models for contacts, companies, addresses, etc. o Lead Management: Define lead stages, sources, and related data. o Opportunity Management: Model sales pipelines, deals, and probabilities. o User Management: Implement user roles, permissions, and authentication. 4. Leverage Laravel Features: o Authentication: Use Laravel's built-in authentication scaffolding to handle user logins and registrations. o Validation: Validate user input to prevent errors and security vulnerabilities. missions, and authentication. 4. This typically includes three major parts:

• 1. The server. This is the computer that receives requests. 2.The app. This is the application running on the server that listens for requests, retrieves information from the database, and sends a response. 3. The database. Databases are used to organize and persist data. How to build a Backend for a Website? Backend involves 1. Define your needs: • how it should work and what core functionalities it should have. • What functionalities will you implement in the future? • How many users will use your app? 2. Choose the right tools • Programming language: This decision will have an effect on all future possibilities and limitations . • API: it is time to specify the type of API (Public, Private, Partner, Composite) and its architecture (REST, SOAP, RPC, GraphQL). It is the massenger that takes request and tells the system what o you want to do and then returns response back to you, it is like a waiter. • API Documentation. • Backend is responsible for creating APIs (Application Programming Interfaces) that allow communication between different components of a system. APIs enable integration with third-party services and ensure a seamless user experience. • Operating system : Then it is good to find the best operating system . Here it is a simple choice: Linux or Windows . In the case of Linux, we need to specify the distribution (RedHat, Fedora, Debian, Ubuntu, etc.) and its version . To avoid being locked into a single operating system, developers often use Docker – a tool that allows them to create a small virtual system just for development purposes. • Building Data base : 3. Deployment: deploy the code into the world • we have many cloud platforms like AWS (Amazon Web Services), Google Cloud, Firebase, DigitalOcean, Heroku, etc. • DevOps have to configure all services you need for the project, eg. ’t Repeat Yourself). 4. In this step, we usually set up two environments: prod (what all can use) and stage (to show changes to the client and for testing purposes).

5. CI/CD: Continuous Integration / Continues Delivery. While CI is responsible for building code and testing it (with automatic tests), CD takes care of the build delivery to the server. Communication Protocols Communications protocols link the frontend to the backend • TCP o is a stream-based connection-oriented protocol . o provides reliable delivery at a cost of connection setup and retransmission • UDP o a message based and connectionless. o starts faster but doesn’t have guaranteed delivery. • There are multiple protocols we have to determine the protocol we will use according to project requirements. • HTTP protocol is built on top or TCP/UDP 2. Accesability along with security compilance (Knowledge of Web Security ). • You can secure communications with encryption or TLS. • You can prevent network intrusion with firewall rules and proper network configuration. You can study common software vulnerabilities and protect against denial of service attacks. As a software engineer, it is important to be familiar with the different types of security risks and how to mitigate them. • One type of security risk is a man-in-the-middle attack. This is where an attacker intercepts communication between two parties and tries to eavesdrop or modify the data being exchanged. To prevent this type of attack, it is important to encrypt and authenticate the communicated parties using Transport Layer Security (TLS). • Another type of security risk is a denial of service attack. This is where an attacker tries to prevent legitimate users from accessing a service by flooding it with requests or through finding a way to crash the backend by sending a special payload.

Another type of security risk is a denial of service attack. This is where an attacker tries to prevent legitimate users from accessing a service by flooding it with requests or through finding a way to crash the backend by sending a special payload. To prevent this type of attack, it is important to have a firewall or a layer 7 DDOS protection layer in place to block illegitimate requests. Cloudflare has great services to detect DDOS traffic. This is of course made possible through layer 7 inspection. • There is a client side security attacks such as XSS (cross side scripting), there are server side security attacks such as SQL injection • Using HTTPS: HTTPS is a secured HTTP. When data is sent using HTTPS, it is protected via the Transport Layer Security protocol, which has three layers of protection: o Encryption: Way of securing data along with a password or “key” which is used to decrypt the information. o Data Integrity: When data integrity is secure, information stored in a database is complete, accurate, and reliable for a lifetime. o Authentication: This method protects against attacks and builds user trust which response to business growth.

• Implementing secure backup and recovery: Data loss could be the major threat that could lead to security breaches. To avoid this, make sure to implement a backup process. This could be implemented in many ways: o Protecting media and content from theft or destruction. o Periodically testing the backups. o Apply a two-person rule so that one person cannot access the content or media without the permission of another article. o and various other methods include XSS Attacks, Software updates, and SQL Injection. 3. Web Server: • Web servers deliver static or dynamic content served on top of the HTTP protocol. • If you build a Web API you may spin up your own web server in your language of choice or use a web framework such as Express or Django. • The internal architecture of web servers can also differ. Web servers can be single or multithreaded. They can have one thread or multiple listener threads. There are many ways client connections can be accepted and distributed among the threads • Examples of off-the-shelf web servers are Apache Tomcat, Apache httpd, and NGINX . The latter can act as both a web server and a proxy. • Modern web servers support both HTTP/1.1 and HTTP/2. HTTP/3 is slowly getting support as its newer protocol. Configuring your web server with the appropriate protocol is critical for performance and resilience of your backend application; And it all depends on the environment. For example, Do you expect multiple concurrent requests from the same client? or do you expect fewer requests but from many clients? Based on that, you can pick multiplexing HTTP/ 2 or vanilla simple HTTP/1.1. • The web server runs websites, it stores, processes, and delivers (response) web pages to the user’s request. When the user makes a request by the web server, it is accepted by an HTTP server which finds and sends back the content to the browser through HTTP. Some examples of web servers are Apache and NGINX and express js which are open-source platforms used to deliver content as per requests made. • When you use Express.js, the web server is essentially built by: 1. Node.js: Provides the underlying HTTP server functionality. 2.

ers are Apache and NGINX and express js which are open-source platforms used to deliver content as per requests made. • When you use Express.js, the web server is essentially built by: 1. Node.js: Provides the underlying HTTP server functionality. 2. Express.js: Handles routing, middleware, and other web application logic on top of Node.js. Like that: • const server = http.createServer((req, res) => { res.statusCode = 200; res.setHeader('Content-Type', 'text/plain'); res.end('Hello, World!\n'); }); 4. Proxy: • Proxies are becoming increasingly popular in the engineering world, especially with the introduction of micro-services. The main purpose of a proxy is it receives requests from a client and forward the requests to backend servers. The proxy hides the network layer identity of the original client from the destination server.

• There are two levels of proxying: layer 4 and layer 7 proxying. Layer 4 proxying works at the transport layer, while layer 7 proxying works at the application level. Each layer provides different capabilities and can be used for different purposes. Layer 7 proxying require the proxy to understand the application protocol while layer 4 proxying works with any application protocol because it works at the transport layer (TCP or UDP). • You might have heard the forward proxy and reverse proxy used interchangeably. Both the forward proxy and the reverse proxy, receive requests from clients and forward the request to a backend. In the forward proxy, the client explicitly asks for a particular backend server and the proxy fulfills this request. Forward proxies must be configured in the client network proxy section for them to work. In the reverse proxy the client doesn’t know the final backend server, to the client the final destination is the reverse proxy. The client doesn’t know there are more servers on the backend. • A common uses cases of proxies are caching, API gateways, authentication, load balancing and much more. For example a content delivery network (CDN) is a reverse proxy that sends request to the origin backends. Fiddler or MITM are proxies configured on the client side and all requests* get sent to them first. Service meshes are essentially a proxy and a reverse proxy. Learn more about proxies here. 5. Containerization & Testing • Containerization is the process of packaging software code with all the necessary elements like frameworks, libraries, and other dependencies, to make services isolated from each other in a container. This process is done by the backend developers to run a container easily. Docker and Kubernetes are the most used containers. • Docker is an open-source containerization platform, that enables developers to package applications into containers. It is similar to a virtual machine but much more efficient. Its basic use of it is it allows the applications to run in different environments.

an open-source containerization platform, that enables developers to package applications into containers. It is similar to a virtual machine but much more efficient. Deployment • When the testing passes, developers move to the deployment where they can see the working of their application. It can also be referred to as a live environment. This process includes 5 steps: Planning, development, testing, deploying, and monitoring. • Planning: When multiple developers are working on the same project, a deployment plan is a necessity. Using this step, you avoid conflicts between changes made making sure the deployment process is as smooth and easy. This could lead to the quicker and more efficient development of the application. • Development: Once, the planning is done, actual development takes place. This is the step where you gather ideas and prioritization requirements. Then, the management of the complete application can be moved to testing.

• Testing: Testing is a crucial step to ensure there are no bugs before moving it to the production level. Testing is possible only when you deploy it to various environments to check whether it fits in or not. Check for the bugs and if any, make sure to remove them and then move to the deployment process. • Deploying: Seeing your application working live, with no bugs, and in an optimized way gives satisfaction to the effort made. It means pushing changes or updates from one environment to another. • Monitoring: Constant monitoring is required to check whether the website is working well or not. There might be a chance that issues may occur after deploying as well, so keep an eye out for the proper functioning, and the time for release should be planned so that minimum users would have viewed and changes can be made to see the optimized web application. 11. Cloud computing can be divided into three major categories, Software as a Service (SaaS), Platform as a Service (PaaS), and Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS). Message Formats

• Message formats go hand in hand with communication protocols; They describe in- wire format of the message being sent. They usually broken down into two types human readable and non-human readable. Examples are XML, JSON and protocol buffers. • When a client sends a message to a backend, it needs to serialize the message from the language data structure to the on-wire message format. When the backend receives the message it needs to then deserialize the message from this format to the language data structure. 19. Message Sytems like kafka and RabbitQA 20. What is a server? • A server is simply a computer that listens for incoming requests. Though there are machines made and optimized for this particular purpose, any computer that is connected to a network can act as a server. In fact, you will often use your very own computer as server when developing apps. Notes: 1. URI: A URI (Uniform Resource Identifier) is a string that refers to a resource.The most common are URLs, which identify the resource by giving its location on the Web. URNs, by contrast, refer to a resource by a name, in a given namespace, such as the ISBN of a book. 2. What are the core functions of the app? The server runs an app that contains logic about how to respond to various requests based on the HTTP verb and the Uniform Resource Identifier (URI). The pair of an HTTP verb and a URI is called a route and matching them based on a request is called routing. Some of these handler functions will be middleware. In this context, middleware is any code that executes between the server receiving a request and sending a response. These middleware functions might modify the request object, query the database, or otherwise process the incoming request. Middleware functions typically end by passing control to the next middleware function, rather than by sending a response. Eventually, a middleware function will be called that ends the request-response cycle by sending an HTTP response back to the client. What is a Proxy?A proxy is essentially an intermediary that acts on behalf of another. General Concept: Imagine you're too busy to go to the grocery store. You ask your friend to go for you.

ponse cycle by sending an HTTP response back to the client. What is a Proxy?A proxy is essentially an intermediary that acts on behalf of another. General Concept: Imagine you're too busy to go to the grocery store. You ask your friend to go for you. Your friend becomes your proxy - they act on your behalf to purchase the groceries. Proxy in Computing: In the digital world, a proxy server acts as an intermediary between your computer and the internet. It handles your internet requests and responds on your behalf. Why use a proxy? Privacy: Hides your IP address, making it harder for websites to track you.

Security: Can filter malicious content or protect against attacks. Caching: Stores copies of frequently accessed web pages to speed up loading times. Circumventing restrictions: Can be used to access blocked websites or content. Types of Proxies:
HTTP Proxy: Handles HTTP requests. SOCKS Proxy: More flexible, allowing for different protocols. Reverse Proxy: Directs traffic to different servers based on rules. Web Server
Directly serves content: Typically handles static content (HTML, CSS, images) and dynamic content generated by applications running on the same server. Focus: Primarily concerned with delivering content to clients. Proxy Server
Intermediary: Acts as a middleman between clients and backend servers. Additional functionalities: Often provides additional services like load balancing, caching, security, and filtering. Flexibility: Can be configured to forward requests to different backend servers based on various criteria. To summarize:
A web server is primarily focused on serving content. A proxy server is more versatile, offering additional features beyond simple request forwarding.

sed on serving content. A proxy server is more versatile, offering additional features beyond simple request forwarding. While a web server can handle basic request forwarding, a proxy server provides more advanced capabilities and is often used in complex architectures. References: •

ting in the memory being “leaked.” 3. There are a number of ways to prevent memory leaks in Node.js. The first is to make sure that all code that allocates memory also frees it. SQL vs NoSQL: SQL: 1. More for read mainly websites. NoSQL: 1. Higher performance due to avoiding ACID transactions. 2. Unstructured data Scalability horizontal better: High-throughput workloads, Real-time analytics: Airbnb : SQL for Structured Data • User profiles: Detailed information about users, including preferences, booking history, and payment details. • Listing information: Property details, location, amenities, pricing. • Booking data: Reservations, cancellations, payments. • Financial transactions: Payment processing, refunds, payouts. NoSQL for Unstructured and Semi-Structured Data • User reviews: Textual reviews and ratings. • Images and videos: Property photos and videos. • Search and recommendation data: User preferences, search history for personalized recommendations. • Real-time analytics: Storing and processing large volumes of user interaction data. • References: 1. https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/the-pros-and-cons-of-node-js-in-web-development/ 2. https://www.cisco.com/c/en/us/solutions/data-center/data-center-networking/what-is-low- latency.html#:~:text=Low%20latency%20is%20the%20ability,provide%20responses%20with %20minimal%20delay . 3. https://phoenixnap.com/glossary/server- deployment#:~:text=Glossary%20%C2%BB%20S%20%C2%BB%20What%20Is%20a,for% 20the%20server's%20intended%20function . 4. https://www.computer.org/publications/tech-news/build-your-career/developers-insight-into- the-ruby-on-rails 5. https://sastra.um.ac.id/wp-content/uploads/2009/11/Reliability.pdf 6. https://www.benchmarkit.solutions/lets-understand-the-pros-and-cons-of-using-django/ 7. https://blog.boot.dev/backend/django-for-backend/ 8. https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/advantages-and-disadvantages-of-php/ Network Security using firewalls, intrusion detection systems, and other measures. Network security is the practice of safeguarding computer networks from unauthorized access, use, disclosure, disruption, modification, or destruction. Funnel Analysis • Definition: A visual representation of the customer journey, from initial awareness to a desired action (e.g., purchase, signup). computer networks from unauthorized access, use, disclosure, disruption, modification, or destruction. Funnel Analysis • Definition: A visual representation of the customer journey, from initial awareness to a desired action (e.g., purchase, signup). • Purpose: To identify bottlenecks and drop-off points in the conversion process, allowing for optimization.

A/B Testing • Definition: Comparing two versions of a webpage or element (e.g., button, headline) to determine which performs better. Heatmaps • Definition: A visual representation of user interactions on a webpage, showing where users click, hover, or scroll. • Purpose: To understand user behavior and identify areas of interest or frustration
